probe: What governs this record's survival — its retention, expiry, quota, or permissions — and WHO OWNS that setting? If the answer is 'nobody, it came that way', the record has no owner and Law 6 is being violated by a default.
fired_at: /sorted, at the completeness gate, whenever a node produces or depends on a record of truth. Also fired once as a standing sweep over tools already in use (see `the_sweep` below).
why:
Law 6 says everything has exactly one owner, and Law 7 says records of truth are permanent. Both are silently broken by UNOWNED DEFAULTS — settings that govern our records which a vendor chose and we inherited. The failure is invisible for a structural reason: **absence of configuration looks exactly like absence of a decision.** Nothing is wrong in any file, no gate goes red, no proof fails. The record simply stops existing on a schedule nobody set.

Akien, 2026-07-22, on the general form: 'tools should carry their intentions and whys and proofs too, at least as far down in the stack as we can... every tool we use, we should prove. Should we prove every library call too? I'mma say not now — but we should have caught the one you mentioned.'
the_stop_rule: THE ANSWER TO 'HOW FAR DOWN' IS NOT DEPTH — IT IS GOVERNANCE. A tool becomes a SEAM when it HOLDS, GATES, or EXPIRES something of ours. Postgres holds a verdict record: seam. The harness expires our transcripts: seam. `json.dumps` produces a value and owns nothing: not a seam, no intention needed — ever, not merely 'not now'. This gives a principled stop instead of a pragmatic one, and it is checkable per TOOL rather than per CALL, so the cost does not grow with the codebase.
yield: UNMEASURED (Law 3). Founding firing count: 1 — the standing sweep below, which found four unowned things on the first pass. That is one firing with a real catch, not a track record.

found: VALIDATIONS — the `cairn` database holds a `validations` table (1 row) with no dump on disk, no cron, no timer, no replica. A record of truth (Law 7: permanent) existing in exactly one place. RULING: the fix is the already-spawned migration of validations out of db_domain to beside-code git, NOT a backup script — git is already durable and already pushed. A backup script would add a second unowned schedule to solve an ownership problem.

TWO LIVE UU CRON JOBS — not dangling symlinks but executing code from the quarry: a curl POST to localhost:8080/api/cc_send every 4 hours, and a nightly 02:00 run of /home/akien/TheIgors/claudecode/run_review_audit.sh. This upgrades the ~/bin debris work from inert to active. Awaiting Akien's ruling on whether either still matters.

CLEANUPPERIODDAYS — unset in ~/.claude/settings.json, so transcripts self-delete at the 30-day default. Oldest transcript here is 2026-07-15; the repo was founded 2026-07-14, so the founding conversations begin erasing around 2026-08-14. Bounded harm: transcripts are load-bearing only while the artifacts fail to hold the why — which they did twice on 2026-07-22, so today they ARE load-bearing.

~/.cairn/devices/sudo_relay/0/{audit,relay} — our own logs, no retention set. DIFFERENT CATEGORY and lower urgency: we own this, so unset is a decision not yet made rather than a default someone else made for us. Worth ruling on, not worth alarm.
the_sweep.not_found_and_that_is_the_measurement: Both git repos measured at 0 unpushed with live GitHub remotes, so everything that lives in git is durable. That is why the migration in finding 1 is the right fix: it moves a record from the un-backed place to the already-backed place, instead of building durability twice.

provenance: The corpus's second DISCOVERED probe (2026-07-22), born the same afternoon as the first. Traced back from a single catch — cleanupPeriodDays — that Akien correctly said we should have caught. Generalizing from one catch is how the probe was found; whether it keeps earning is what tenure measures (T5.1).
